claim_chain: Hoverfly optics, photoreceptors, and HS cells exhibit clear sexual dimorphism
Optic flow sensitive descending neurons show sexually dimorphic velocity response functions
Despite multi-level anatomical and neural sexual dimorphism, WBA and head movements remain mostly monomorphic
Differences between neural and behavioural velocity response functions highlight likely involvement of downstream processing mechanisms
Sensory processing differences emerge at lower velocities than the velocities where motor output diverges
Findings suggest a complex transformation between sex-specific sensory encoding and conserved motor behaviour

typical_pattern: Present behavioural findings showing monomorphism or dimorphism
Discuss broader implications for flight control and evolutionary tuning

likely_priorities: Direct evidence linking descending neurons to specific motor outputs
Quantification of additional kinematic parameters beyond wingbeat amplitude
Recording from multiple viewing angles to capture nuanced wing and body movements
Identification of downstream integration mechanisms reconciling dimorphic sensory input with monomorphic motor output
Validation in free-flight conditions rather than tethered setup
likely_attacks: Lack of direct evidence implicating OFS DNs in wingbeat amplitude control
Technical limitation of recording from above missing head movements to roll, lift or thrust
WBA recordings affected by filming angle lacking other nuanced changes in wing angles
Tethered flight setup may not reveal biomechanical advantages of smaller male body size
Contributions from descending neurons beyond the two characterized not fully explored
No quantification of abdominal or haltere movements
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly acknowledges multiple technical and interpretive limitations: 'there is currently no direct evidence implicating them in the control of wingbeat amplitude'; 'this could be a technical limitation of recording from above'; 'Our WBA recordings would also be affected by filming from above and thus lacking other nuanced changes in wing angles'; 'While we did not quantify abdominal or haltere movements'; 'biomechanical advantages...which do not appear in a tethered flight set-up'. The authors also note that 'It is thus likely that the WBA that we recorded here reflects contributions from descending neurons beyond the two that we have characterised', indicating awareness that their characterization is incomplete.

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, invented numbers, or author names were included. Reviewer preferences were inferred solely from the paper's own stated limitations and caveats.
